flashcards.back: A method of evaluating algorithm efficiency by examining how resource requirements (time/space) grow as input size approaches infinity, ignoring constant factors and lower-order terms.

flashcards.front: What does Big O notation (O) represent?
flashcards.back: The upper bound (worst-case growth rate) of an algorithm. It describes the maximum time or space an algorithm needs for any input of size n.

flashcards.front: What does Big Omega notation (Ω) represent?
flashcards.back: The lower bound (best-case growth rate) of an algorithm. It describes the minimum time or space an algorithm requires for any input of size n.

flashcards.front: What does Big Theta notation (Θ) represent?
flashcards.back: The tight bound - it represents both the upper and lower bounds, meaning the algorithm's growth rate is asymptotically bounded both above and below.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(1)?
flashcards.back: Constant time - the algorithm takes the same amount of time regardless of input size. Example: array index access.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(log n)?
flashcards.back: Logarithmic time - time increases slowly as input grows. Example: binary search, where problem size reduces by half each step.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(n)?
flashcards.back: Linear time - time increases proportionally with input size. Example: simple linear search, iterating through an array once.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(n log n)?
flashcards.back: Linearithmic time - common in efficient sorting algorithms like Merge Sort and Heap Sort.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(n²)?
flashcards.back: Quadratic time - time grows with the square of input size. Example: nested loops, bubble sort, insertion sort.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(2^n)?
flashcards.back: Exponential time - time doubles with each increase in input size. Example: recursive Fibonacci, subset generation.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of O(n!)?
flashcards.back: Factorial time - the worst complexity, growing factorially. Example: generating all permutations, traveling salesman problem brute force.
The amount of memory space an algorithm needs as a function of input size, including the input itself and auxiliary space used.

flashcards.front: What is the Master Theorem used for?
flashcards.back: It provides a solution for recurrence relations of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a≥1, b>1, used to analyze divide-and-conquer algorithms.
A method analyzing average time per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations, ensuring average performance even if some operations are expensive.
